anchored_threads.thread_name: Karma Yoga / disciplined action (verses 2.31-2.53). How to act once one knows the substrate.
anchored_threads.anchor_evidence: 2.36 sits inside the karma-yoga block opener (Shankara's pentad 2.33-2.37) and closes the disgrace argument that begins at 2.31. The engineering layer respects this by mapping the verse onto the specific failure mode the karma-yoga discipline addresses: retreat-under-social-pressure when the technical case for action is sound. The chapter thesis's 'duty is not optional, even when it is hard' is honored exactly — the engineer is given a tool (named-narrator analysis) to recognize the social-cost dimension as a real cost, without using the social cost to invent a technical conclusion.

progression_issue.explanation: The verse is the third in the disgrace-argument sequence (2.34-2.36) before the symmetric-outcome pivot at 2.37. The engineering layer respects this sequential intensification — 2.34 (durable ill-fame) → 2.35 (formerly-esteemed retraction) → 2.36 (enemies' indecent words about earned prowess) — and identifies 2.36 as the rhetorical climax. The 'tato duḥkhataraṁ nu kim' is preserved as the verse's structural climax, not flattened into general peer-pressure commentary.
